description: The tough outer covering of a tree's trunk, branches, and roots. 'Koremu' names the tree's armor and skin—the textured surface that protects living wood, records years of growth, and provides habitat for countless small beings.
sound_symbolism: The word covers and protects. 'Ko' begins with firm velar closure, the solid covering. Then 're' roughens with texture, the bark's varied surface. Finally 'mu' embraces with nasal enclosure, the protective wrapping complete.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun: 'lo koremu' (the bark). Takes the vegetal classifier 'lipha' when counted. Related to 'shiro' (tree) as its outer layer. Used in contexts of protection, texture, and traditional medicine/craft materials.

pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Bark's varied textures fascinated Art Nouveau artists—the organic patterns, the play of light on rough surfaces, nature's own decorative art.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Bark provided medicine, dyes, cordage, and even canoes—traditional peoples knew each tree's bark and its gifts.
